entry.title: Discover SAP Business Technology Platform at Spend Connect Live
entry.content:
Spend Connect Live and Spend Connect Virtual are quickly approaching, and I can hardly contain my excitement! There you will be able to see the application of SAP Business Technology Platform (SAP BTP) through expert sessions and customers stories. To prepare, here’s a summary of what SAP Business Technology Platform will be showing.

In the world of procurement, businesses need to find ways to make smarter spend decisions. According to a recent IDC research study sponsored by SAP, Overall, companies saw a 495% three-year return, taking only an average of six months to pay back on their investment by improving cross-organizational efficiencies, creating value through use of data, and more using SAP BTP.

Discover how SAP BTP can impact your Spend systems

If you’re new to SAP Business Technology Platform, Spend Connect Live has several sessions and resources that can help you discover SAP BTP. Our BTP101: Intro to SAP Business Technology Platform session can help you understand how SAP BTP optimizes your procurement processes. You can futher expand on the applications of SAP BTP and view use cases relating to procurement, business network, and supply chain in the BTP201: Explore business scenarios enabled by SAP BTP session. For those interested in Concur, Streamline T&E management with integration of SAP and SAP Concur solutions will highlight how automation and integration between your Concur and ERP solutions can be done using SAP BTP.

Still have questions? You can visit us at the SAP Business Technology Platform Demo Pod on the show floor for additional demos and time with experts.

Hear directly from SAP customers how they benefit from SAP BTP

Now that you know what SAP BTP can do with spend solutions, you’re most likely now wondering how you can apply it to your business. Here are some notable customer use cases you can read, as well as some of the customer success stories being presented at Spend Connect Live.

Customer use case: Infosys, a leading provider improving supplier management

Infosys, an SAP customer, partner, and a global leader in technology consulting, has also improved supplier management. By using SAP BTP, Infosys were able to develop four custom applications for suppliers that allowed the integration of their many SAP solutions. With over 3,000 of their suppliers onboarded, they were able to decrease the onboarding time by 50%—all with one interface. Suppliers are now able to effectively use SAP Business Network for their needs, and Infosys can improve their supplier relations and processes.

Customer use case: Blue Diamond Growers, the biggest almond processor in the world optimizing key processes

Blue Diamond Growers  is the largest almond processor and marketer in the world, and are utilizing SAP BTP to support their over 3,000 suppliers and 1,800 employees by providing real-time data using SAP Analytics Cloud and automating manual processes using SAP Build Process Automation. Together with SAP Integration Suite, they are able to optimize key processes across procurement, inventory management, and quality control.

Spend Connect Live session: How Richemont enhanced supplier collaboration

At Spend Connect Live there are customer sessions highlighting how SAP BTP has elevated their procurement processes. At the session Growing supplier collaboration at Richemont to gain supply chain visibility, you can see firsthand how luxury goods group Richemont has contributed their improved performance to enhanced supplier collaboration using SAP BTP.

Spend Connect Live session: How Endress+Hauser digitized their asset lifestyle

Another customer session to note is Transforming asset collaboration at Endress+Hauser. At this session you can learn how the global leader of automations and measurement technology has digitized their asset lifecycle using SAP BTP, and they will be showcasing the benefits of using a platform along with SAP Business Network Asset Collaboration.  

Spend Connect Live session: Sony improves their cost management

Finally, technology conglomerate Sony will be presenting in Optimizing efficiency at Sony with SAP Ariba solutions and SAP BTP, where they will showcase how they utilized source-to-pay SAP Ariba solutions and SAP BTP to improve their cost management.

Learn more about SAP BTP for Spend and more virtually at Spend Connect Virtual

With SAP BTP being a unified cloud-based platform, it can optimize the processes for both companies and their suppliers, whether it be by improving visibility into procurement operations or integrating solutions for a seamless experience. As your business grows, you will need a platform that grows with you—Be sure to attend or watch the BTP101 and BTP201 sessions for the foundational knowledge of SAP BTP for spend. In anticipation for Spend Connect Live and Spend Connect Virtual you can learn more about SAP BTP for Spend at sap.com/btp-for-spend.

I’m looking forward to seeing you all at Spend Connect Live! Can’t make it in person? Register for Spend Connect Virtual here.

entry.title: The Rise of AI Agents: Transforming Business and Beyond
entry.content:
AI Agents

As someone deeply interested in the field of artificial intelligence, I’ve had the privilege of observing its remarkable evolution firsthand. Today, I want to share some insights into how AI agents are not only reshaping the business landscape but are also poised to transform the way we live and work.

 

Open AI 5 Levels to Reach AGI

The AI Revolution: More Than Just Hype

We are in the midst of a true AI revolution. What started with simple chatbots has rapidly evolved into highly sophisticated systems that touch almost every aspect of our daily lives and work environments. In just the past few weeks, we’ve seen groundbreaking advancements, particularly in tools developed by leading AI companies like OpenAI. These innovations are pushing the boundaries of what AI can do—creating more intelligent, reliable, and efficient systems that are becoming essential to modern businesses.

Enter AI Agents: The Next Frontier in Automation

One of the most exciting developments in AI is the rise of AI agents. Unlike traditional chatbots that are limited to scripted conversations, AI agents are complex systems capable of reasoning, learning, and autonomously executing tasks with minimal human input. These agents can communicate naturally, connect to external applications (like CRM systems or email platforms), and even make decisions based on real-time data from the company. Source

What does this mean for businesses? AI agents can streamline operations, improve customer interactions, and transform problem-solving processes across industries. The shift from chatbots to AI agents marks a significant leap forward—one that many industry experts believe will redefine the workforce. In fact, some predict that by 2025, there could be more AI agents operating in the workplace than human employees. Source

How to Work with AI Agents: A Human-Centered Approach

As AI continues to advance, it’s important to approach it with the right mindset. While AI agents can handle increasingly complex tasks, the human element remains critical to ensure these systems function optimally and ethically. Here’s a practical framework for working with AI:

 

  • Always include AI in decision-making processes: AI agents can offer insights that might not be immediately obvious to humans. Bringing them into the conversation early allows businesses to leverage their full potential.
  • Be the human in the loop: Even with highly autonomous AI agents, human oversight is essential. This ensures that systems are aligned with business goals and can adapt to changes in real-time.
  • Treat AI as a collaborator, not just a tool: The concept of anthropomorphism—treating AI as a 'partner' rather than just software—helps foster better interaction and integration within teams.
  • Stay adaptable: AI technology is advancing at a breakneck pace. What is cutting-edge today could be outdated tomorrow. Businesses must remain flexible, continually adopting new tools and approaches to stay competitive.

 

Source

What Sets AI Agents Apart from Traditional Chatbots?

It’s essential to understand what makes AI agents different from the chatbots we’ve all grown accustomed to. Traditional chatbots typically operate within set rules and scripts, handling basic customer service queries or directing users to the right resources.

AI agents, however, are much more powerful. Here are some key features that distinguish AI agents from chatbots:

 

  • Powered by advanced natural language processing (NLP): AI agents can understand and respond in more human-like ways, making interactions smoother and more intuitive.
  • Connected to outside applications: They don’t just engage in conversations—they can connect with external systems like CRM, supply chain management, and email platforms to perform tasks.
  • Reasoning and planning: AI agents can analyze situations and develop plans based on real-time data, allowing them to make informed decisions autonomously.
  • Access to company data: They can be fine-tuned using company-specific data, making their outputs more relevant and effective.
  • Autonomous execution of tasks: Beyond just offering suggestions, AI agents can execute tasks semi-autonomously, such as processing orders or responding to customer queries.
  • Continuous learning and improvement: These systems are designed to learn from their interactions and continuously improve over time.

 

Source

The Road Ahead: AI’s Role in the Future of Work

The future of AI is incredibly bright, with applications that will continue to disrupt traditional business models. From improving customer experiences to automating complex workflows, AI agents are already reshaping industries. But this is just the beginning.

As AI agents become more capable, business leaders and professionals need to embrace these changes. Adopting AI tools and integrating them into core business functions isn’t just an option—it’s a necessity for staying competitive in a rapidly evolving marketplace.

One thing is clear: AI is no longer just a futuristic concept. It’s here now, transforming the way we live and work. By understanding its capabilities and learning how to work alongside AI agents, we can unlock new levels of innovation, efficiency, and success in our organizations.

Conclusion: How Will AI Agents Impact Your Industry?

The rise of AI agents presents exciting opportunities and challenges across industries. Whether you’re in finance, healthcare, retail, or any other sector, the impact of these systems will be profound.

What are your thoughts on the rise of AI agents? How do you see them changing your industry? I’d love to hear your perspectives—let’s continue the conversation in the comments.

#ArtificialIntelligence #BusinessInnovation #FutureOfWork #AIAgents #Automation #slithoughtleadership #sapbusinessAI

entry.title: Talk to Your Data: A Gen AI use case with SAP HANA Cloud vector engine and SAP generative AI hub
entry.content:
Introduction:

In continuing the exploration of innovative SAP HANA Cloud vector engine implementations, we now turn to another remarkable example from LeverX. LeverX from SAP Partner Ecosystem has developed an innovative solution as part of the SAP BTP Gen AI Lighthouse Program. Continuing from Wipro and Deloitte's success stories, this blog explores how LeverX leverages the SAP HANA Cloud vector engine to transform operations and boost efficiency across industries. LeverX has implemented the Unstructured Data Intelligent Assistant solution, addressing challenges in searching and analyzing unstructured data stored in custom formats, helping companies streamline and optimize production design processes.

Use Case: Unstructured Data Intelligent Assistant

Problem Statement:

Most companies face challenges in effectively searching, navigating, and analyzing unstructured or custom data stored in various formats such as CAD and PDX. They struggle with the lack of embedded tools for searching through local custom repositories and cloud data storage containing unstructured or packed data. Additionally, data management issues, such as the creation of duplicated data instead of reusing existing templates, further complicate the process. Complex tools make it harder for users to use natural language prompts for efficient data search and analysis across both SAP and non-SAP platforms.

Solution:

LeverX designed an Unstructured Data Intelligent Assistant solution on the SAP BTP platform that, according to the company, seamlessly integrates ERP data sources, utilizing efficient data preparation methods and harnessing the SAP HANA Cloud vector engine for advanced storage and vector-based search capabilities. It leverages SAP BTP's request processing layer, utilizing the SAP Generative AI Hub to transform natural language prompts into SQL queries and performs vector search against vectorized information in HANA Cloud, enabling efficient and accurate data retrieval. The processed data is then visualized and made accessible through a flexible Intelligent Assistant component.

The Intelligent Assistant operates in two modes: Standalone Mode and Embedded Mode. In Standalone Mode, it functions independently, providing powerful search and analysis capabilities through APIs, allowing users to interact with unstructured data across various platforms. In Embedded Mode, it integrates seamlessly within SAP systems, enhancing the ability to search and analyze unstructured data directly within the SAP environment.

This dual-mode functionality ensures that the Intelligent Assistant can be integrated across different workflows and systems, whether on SAP or non-SAP platforms. By supporting both standalone and embedded deployment, this solution addresses the lack of ready-to-go tools in the market, significantly reducing the effort required to search through files in proprietary formats.

LeverX created the solution to help non-technical users analyze proprietary data more effectively, support better data management practices, and reduce duplication through the reuse of existing templates to enhance operational efficiency and decision-making processes.

Solution Overview:

Reference Architecture:

 

Value Add of SAP HANA Cloud vector engine:

The SAP HANA Cloud Vector Engine plays a crucial role in the Intelligent Assistant solution by enhancing the capabilities of searching and analyzing unstructured data. Here’s how it is used in this context:

  • Vector-Based Search Capabilities: The SAP HANA Cloud vector engine powers similarity searches by interpreting the semantic meaning of queries, making it more effective in retrieving relevant information from unstructured data like PDFs, CAD files, and PDX files, compared to traditional keyword searches.
  • Handling Unstructured Data: Unstructured data often lacks a predefined schema, making it challenging to search and analyze using conventional methods. The vector engine addresses this challenge by supporting the storage and querying of vectors (obtained from embedding the unstructured data) that capture the context and relationships, enabling accurate and efficient searches even with large and varied datasets.
  • Enhanced Data Retrieval: The vector similarity search capability allows the Intelligent Assistant to find data based on conceptual similarity rather than exact matches, making searches with natural language prompts more intuitive and user-friendly.
  • Integration with Natural Language Processing: The vector engine works in conjunction with the SAP generative AI hub, which processes natural language queries into SQL and performs vector search against vectorized information in HANA Cloud. The vector engine performs the search and retrieval process, allowing users to ask complex questions in plain language. It provides precise answers, even when the underlying data is unstructured and distributed across different repositories.
  • Integration Across SAP and Non-SAP Systems: The vector engine's integration with SAP BTP allows for seamless access and retrieval of data across both SAP and non-SAP systems, ensuring comprehensive search capabilities across all relevant data sources.

Conclusion:

Our partners, such as Wipro, Deloitte, and LeverX, are leveraging the SAP HANA Cloud vector engine and SAP BTP services to create innovative solutions that drive real value for our joint customers across various industries. These collaborations showcase how the power of SAP technologies is transforming business operations and enabling new levels of efficiency and insight. From enhancing AI-driven solutions to improving data management and analytics, each partner's use case has showcased the transformative potential of using SAP HANA Cloud to power intelligent data applications. With the insights shared in this series, I hope to have provided a comprehensive understanding of how SAP HANA Cloud can be leveraged to solve real-world challenges. Keep an eye out for future events as we keep exploring the exciting and ever-changing world of technology powered by the in-memory database.

Note: This blog is a collaborative effort between SAP HANA Cloud Product Management and LeverX, highlighting the innovative solution developed by the LeverX team: Vitali Usau (vitali.usau@leverx.com), Dzmtry Buriak (dzmitry.buriak@leverx.com), Pavel Lazhbanau (pavel.lazhbanau@leverx.com), Siarhei Valenda (siarhei.valenda@leverx.com). For those interested in learning more or exploring implementation details, please feel free to reach out to the mentioned team member contacts.

References:

  1. Try for yourself:
    • Explore SAP HANA Cloud Vector Engine via Basic Trial
    • Harnessing Generative AI Capabilities with SAP HANA Cloud Vector Engine
    • Develop a CAP-based (multitenant) application using GenAI and Retrieval Augmented Generation
  2. Read about other partner use cases leveraging SAP BTP's Generative AI capabilities
    • SAP Lighthouse Program - Partner Use Cases adopting BTP GenAI Capabilities [Part 1]
    • SAP Lighthouse Program - Partner Use Cases adopting BTP GenAI Capabilities [Part 2]
    • SAP Lighthouse Program - Partner Use Cases adopting BTP GenAI Capabilities [Part 3]

entry.title: Low-Code Machine Learning with Ludwig and SAP AI Core #2-Defect Detection & Sound Anomaly Detection
entry.content:
In my previous blog post, we have discussed the business values, overview and technical details about end-to-end Low-Code Machine Learning with Ludwig and SAP AI Core. In this blog post, I'll continue sharing my experience of replicating another two custom AI use cases from previous work steam with ludwig in low-code approach, comparing with their original implementations in pro-code approach.

Use Case#2: Defect Detection with Image Segmentation

In this use case, we use computer vision(image segmentation) for defect detection in production line of Light Guide Plate. Please refer to our blog post about Build Your Own Model with TensorFlow in SAP AI Core for defect detection and source code for more details. The original implementation is a U-Net model architecture with Tensorflow by my colleague cesarecalabria.

The original training code is available here with 483 lines of python code. Here is just a screenshot of some code snippet. In order to implement this training code, it not only require you to have a good understanding the unet model architecture, but also the skill of ML development with python and tensorflow.

Okay, no worry. Let's implement the same model based on the same dataset with Ludwig in a much easier way.

Dataset and Data Pre-Process

The original dataset is structured as below. The original images of products are stored in Images folder, their corresponding segmented image are store in Masks folder. Sub folders NG(Defected Product) and OK(Passed) are labels of the product image, which is not in used here.

Every product image in Images folder has its own masked pair image in Masks folder. For example

data/Images/NG/00000_NG_Image.bmpdata/Masks/NG/00000_NG_Mask.bmp
Prdocut ImageMask of Defect

The original images in the dataset has a bmp format, which isn't supported in ludwig image feature, hence, we have to transform the original images from bmp format into jpg. Here is the sample code below.

 

import os
from PIL import Image

def convert_bmp_to_jpg(directory):
    # Walk through all files in the directory and its subdirectories
    for root, dirs, files in os.walk(directory):
        for file in files:
            if file.endswith(".bmp"):
                # Create the full file path
                bmp_path = os.path.join(root, file)

                # Open the BMP image
                with Image.open(bmp_path) as img:
                    # Convert the image to RGB mode
                    img = img.convert("RGB")

                    # Create the new file name with .jpg extension
                    jpg_path = os.path.splitext(bmp_path)[0] + ".jpg"

                    # Save the image as a JPG
                    img.save(jpg_path, "JPEG")

                # Optionally, delete the original BMP file after conversion
                os.remove(bmp_path)

                print(f"Converted {bmp_path} to {jpg_path}")

# Replace './data' with the actual path to your directory
convert_bmp_to_jpg("./data")

 

 In addition, we'll also need to create a csv file as dataset.csv based on the image dataset like

 

image_path,mask_path,label
data/Images/NG/00000_NG_Image.jpg,data/Masks/NG/00000_NG_Mask.jpg,NG
data/Images/NG/00001_NG_Image.jpg,data/Masks/NG/00001_NG_Mask.jpg,NG

 

Here is the python script to generate dataset.csv based on the folder structure of dataset

 

import os
import csv

# Define paths
image_dir = "data/Images"
mask_dir = "data/Masks"
output_csv = "dataset.csv"

# Collect all image and mask file paths
image_files = {"NG": [], "OK": []}
mask_files = {"NG": [], "OK": []}

for label in ["NG", "OK"]:
    image_files[label] = sorted([os.path.join(image_dir, label, f) for f in os.listdir(os.path.join(image_dir, label))])
    mask_files[label] = sorted([os.path.join(mask_dir, label, f) for f in os.listdir(os.path.join(mask_dir, label))])

# Create and write to the CSV file
with open(output_csv, mode="w", newline="") as file:
    writer = csv.writer(file)
    writer.writerow(["image", "mask", "label"])

    for label in ["NG", "OK"]:
        for img, mask in zip(image_files[label], mask_files[label]):
            writer.writerow([img, mask, label])

print(f"Dataset CSV file has been created: {output_csv}")

 

 

A declarative config.yaml

Based on the dataset above, all the code we need to train a defect detection model of LPG product with image segmentation is a declarative config.yaml as below. Please refer to this for more details about image feature in ludwig. Literally, we have simplified the training from 480 lines of python code into 40 lines of declarative yaml code. It is way too easy and straight-forward, even application developer and business users with limited machine learning knowledge can write such a declarative config.yam. Having said that, you still need the skill of data pre-processing, and the knowledge of selecting the input and output features based on business objectives and dataset.

 

input_features:
  - name: image_path
    type: image
    preprocessing:
      num_processes: 6
      height: 224
      width: 224
      infer_image_max_height: 1024
      infer_image_max_width: 1024
    encoder: unet

output_features:
  - name: mask_path
    type: image
    preprocessing:
      num_processes: 6
      height: 224
      width: 224
      infer_image_max_height: 1024
      infer_image_max_width: 1024
      infer_image_num_classes: true
      num_channels: 1
      num_classes: 2
    decoder:
      type: unet
      num_fc_layers: 0
    loss:
      type: softmax_cross_entropy
    metric:
      type: iou

combiner:
  type: concat
  num_fc_layers: 0

trainer:
  epochs: 100
  early_stop: -1
  batch_size: 1
  max_batch_size: 1

 

For the model training and serving, you can just use the ludwig command lines, like ludwig experiment/serve etc. which we have gone through in last blog post in detail. So are running the same ludwig workloads within SAP AI Core.

Pro-Code VS Low-Code

Next, let's have a look another use case about audio features.

Use Case#3: Predictive Maintenance based on Machinery Sound Anomaly Detection

In the last use case, we'll detect the sound anomaly of machinery for predictive maintenance of production line. Please refer to our blog post about Sound-based predictive maintenance with SAP AI Core and SAP AI Launchpad and source code for more details. The original implementation is a custom CNN model with Tensorflow by my colleague amagnani.

The original training code is available here with 345 lines of python code. Here is just a screenshot of some code snippet. In order to implement this training code, it not only require you to have a good understanding of CNN, but also the skill of ML development with python and tensorflow.

Okay, no worry. Let's implement the same model based on the same dataset with Ludwig in a much easier way.

Dataset and Data Pre-Process

The original dataset is structured as below. The sub folders anomaly1, anomaly2 and ok are the labels of the audio samples.

We'll also need to create a csv file as dataset.csv based on the image dataset like 

 

audio_path,class
data/ok/clip0568.wav,ok
data/anomaly1/clip0583.wav,anomaly1
data/anomaly2/clip0597.wav,anomaly2

 

Here is the python script to generate dataset.csv based on the folder structure of dataset 

 

import os
import csv

# Define the root directory and the output CSV file
root_dir = "data"
output_csv = "dataset.csv"

# Prepare the data list
data = []

# Traverse the directory structure
for subdir, dirs, files in os.walk(root_dir):
    for file in files:
        if file.endswith(".wav"):  # Filter for .wav files
            # Construct the file path and class label
            file_path = os.path.join(subdir, file)
            class_label = os.path.basename(subdir)
            data.append([file_path, class_label])

# Write to the CSV file
with open(output_csv, "w", newline="") as csvfile:
    writer = csv.writer(csvfile)
    writer.writerow(["audio_path", "class"])  # Write header
    writer.writerows(data)

print(f"CSV file '{output_csv}' generated successfully.")

 

A declarative config.yaml

Based on the dataset above, all the code we need to train a sound anomaly detection with CNN is a declarative config.yaml as below. Please refer to this for more details about image feature in ludwig. Literally, we have simplified the training from 345 lines of python code into 19 lines of declarative yaml code. It is much easier and more straight-forward, even application developers and business users with limited machine learning knowledge can write such a declarative config.yam. Having said that, you still need the skill of data pre-processing, and the knowledge of selecting the input and output features based on business objectives and dataset.

 

input_features:
  - name: audio_path
    type: audio
    preprocessing:
      audio_file_length_limit_in_s: 7.5
      audio_feature:
        type: stft
        window_length_in_s: 0.04
        window_shift_in_s: 0.02
    encoder:
      type: parallel_cnn

output_features:
  - name: class
    type: category

trainer:
  epochs: 100
  early_stop: -1

 

Pro-Code VS Low-Code

Conclusion

In these two use cases, we have witnessed the ease and efficiency of the low-code machine learning with Ludwig and SAP AI Core over the pro-code approach. Ludwig simplifies the machine learning process with a low-code approach. Not only opening the door of machine learning for citizen ML developers like application developer, even business user etc. but also increasing the productivity of the professional machine learning engineers and data scientists to quickly experiment, build, train, and deploy models. When paired with SAP AI Core, these machine learning operations can be streamlined and managed at scale, fostering faster AI-driven innovation across the enterprise with enterprise-grade of security, scalability and compliance.

entry.title: SAP Business AI Testing = SAP API Testing
entry.content:
Introduction - why testing APIs is important for SAP Business AI 

When we talk about SAP Business AI in business we need to remember one thing: AI cannot work without APIs. In fact, testing SAP Business AI is all about testing how well APIs work. Let’s break down why this is so important.

Figure 1 - generative AI cannot do any business activity without SAP APIs 

Only AI powered Chatbots in SAP Business AI? 

SAP Business AI has a potential to eliminate all frontend applications (SAP GUI, Fiori and all other front end applications) and leave only chatbots (Joule, Copilot, etc.) to work with all SAP business functions of SAP S/4HANA. This might mean that all business functions will be exposed via APIs. 

Figure 2 - SAP Gui - sailing away into the past 

Why APIs Are Important for SAP Business AI

APIs (Application Programming Interfaces) are like bridges that let different programs talk to each other. AI, especially advanced AI like generative AI, needs APIs to get information and connect with other systems. Without these connections, AI can't do much.

Integration is the backbone of digital transformations, and APIs are the building blocks for every SAP customer who sells, buys, or transports their products. This means APIs are the key to making all of a company’s systems work together. For AI, APIs are what allow it to be part of that big picture.

How APIs Help SAP Business AI Work

Think of AI like a chef who needs ingredients to make a meal. The API is the helper that brings those ingredients to the kitchen. Without it, the chef (AI) can’t do their job. Here’s how APIs help AI:

  1. Getting Data: AI needs a lot of information to make smart decisions. APIs bring that data from different places to the AI.
  2. Executing Business Activities: To do any business task—like creating an order, delivering goods, or managing inventory—AI needs to use APIs to make things happen. For example, if AI is asked to create a new sales order, it has to send a request through an API to the system that manages orders. Without APIs, AI wouldn’t be able to carry out any business activities.
  3. Building New Solutions: APIs allow businesses to create new smart tools by connecting AI with existing software, kind of like mixing different ingredients to create a new recipe.

Figure 3 - SAP API Testing with Int4 Suite = SAP Business AI testing 

How does SAP Business AI execute business activities on SAP S/4HANA

SAP has many ways to expose it’s SAP S/4HANA and their use depends on the context:

  1. SAP APIs (Webservices, ODATA, etc.) can be exposed directly from SAP S/4HANA. Some typical ways include generating web services or ODATA interfaces.
  2. In order to manage APIs exposed by SAP S/4HANA some other components might be used like SAP BTP API Management (part of SAP Integration Suite) and those APIs can also be exposed by SAP Business Accelerator Hub – https://api.sap.com
  3. In most cases however SAP APIs are build and designed using SAP Integration Suite. SAP Integration Suite is the go to solution for building the SAP S/4HANA Clean Core side by side extensions which include building and orchestrating complex integration scenarios and exposing them to the outside world (to SAP Business AI, Joule, Copilots, etc.)

Why Testing SAP Business AI = Testing SAP APIs

Since APIs are so important to AI, testing SAP Business AI is really about testing how well the APIs work. If the APIs don’t work right, the AI won’t either. Here are some key things to check when testing APIs:

  1. Getting and Posting the Right Data: Making sure the API sends the right information to the AI, so it can make the best decisions and making sure that APIs exposed to AI are working as they we designed
  2. Working Together: Testing if AI and other SAP systems can communicate smoothly through APIs.
  3. Speed and Performance: Ensuring the APIs work quickly and don’t slow down the AI’s responses.

In short, AI can’t exist without APIs. And in SAP systems, this connection is even more important because everything is linked together through APIs. So, if you're testing SAP Business AI, you're really testing the APIs that make it all possible.

Further Reference

Learn how to test SAP APIs with Int4 Suite - Avoid SAP S/4HANA Project Delays with Third-Party Systems Service Virtualization

entry.title: From Revenue and Demand Planning to MRP Production Planning – A functional view
entry.content:
In this first article, we will explore how revenue planning and volume/demand planning in SAP Analytics Cloud could work together with Material Requirement Planning (MRP) process and production planning in SAP S/4HANA, from the business process point of view, focusing particularly on small and medium enterprises requirements. Understanding this functional integration can help businesses improve efficiency, reduce costs, and achieve their strategic goals. In a next article, we will deep-dive into the technical details of this integration.

The E2E process to cover

In today's competitive market, businesses need efficient planning and execution to stay ahead. At SAP, some services and business applications like SAP Analytics Cloud, SAP Business Technology Platform, and SAP S/4HANA offer powerful capabilities to integrate revenue planning with demand planning, production planning and execution, ensuring smooth and effective operations.

  1. Revenue/Financial Planning in SAP Analytics Cloud allows businesses to forecast product/services sales, analyze market trends, and create accurate financial plans. This helps companies allocate resources wisely and make informed decisions based on 360° analytics, simulation, and predictive analytics.
  2. Volume/Demand planning in SAP Analytics Cloud involves the process of forecasting and planning the quantities of products or services that will be required to meet customer demand and revenue targets, optimally derived from the Revenue Planning process in the previous step.
  3. Production Planning in Material Requirements Planning (MRP), using Planned Independent Requirements (PIRs) in SAP S/4HANA, ensures that manufacturing processes align with demand forecasts from previous steps. The PIRs are generated based on the demand forecast for the finished products. These PIRs are used as input for the MRP process, which then calculates the material requirements for the finished products' production. The MRP process also takes into account the lead times, inventory levels, and lot sizing rules to determine the quantities and timing of material orders.

In a nutshell, by combining revenue and demand planning (in quantities) with MRP-driven production planning, businesses can better align their operations with market demand. Accurate revenue and demand forecasts from SAP Analytics Cloud inform the MRP process in SAP S/4HANA, leading to efficient production plans and resource use. This end-to-end integration helps reducing production delays, avoid excess inventory, and meet customer demand promptly.

More details

From Revenue Planning ...

As already known from many existing articles, SAP Analytics Cloud is a real toolkit offering unlimited modelling capabilities that enable any type of planning process to be created, and bridging all lines of businesses together (from Finance, to Sales, to HR, to Manufacturing, etc..) in an Extend Planning and Analytics (xP&A) platform. As such, a revenue planning process performed there would involve forecasting future product revenue streams to support strategic decision-making and ensure financial stability.

... to Demand/Volume Planning ...

This above process can also incorporate product pricing information into its modeling. And by having projections of future revenues by product (and other planning axis such as Regions or Customers for instance), as well as the product selling prices, demand in terms of volume can be easily derived (Volume = Revenue/Price). For sure, volume itself can also be planned independently, which would adjust the Revenue accordingly. In this case, we would talk about Consensus Net Revenue. Selling prices can even be simulated along the planning axis, which can also be included in the revenue planning application if needed.

Next, supply chain managers must allocate this volume (as total demand in quantities including inventory requirements) to production sites, considering manufacturing constraints. This step is optimally done in SAP Integrated Business Planning (SAP IBP), which can represent a huge added value for large customers with large product portfolio and complex production processes.

But, in this article, we are addressing a simpler view for smaller customers with simpler supply chains. And by leveraging SAP Analytics Cloud, supply chain managers can easily maintain production constraint drivers and leverage the built-in powerful allocation engine to distribute and propagate the demand plan to manufacturing locations. 

… to Production Planning (in S/4 HANA)

Finally, Demand and Supply must reconcile, and the output of this reconciliation must be translated into a production plan, and transferred to SAP S/4HANA, as an expected demand forecast in the form of a Planning Independent Requirement (PIRs). This step is quite important as demand /PIRs represent demand for a product in a given supplying location. So we must ensure that volume plan is issued at the product / manufacturing location level, in order to match the granularity of the PIRs.

As highlighted above, PIRs are primary inputs for the MRP process, and represent anticipated demand for a product that is not directly linked to specific sales orders. During the MRP runs, the system considers PIRs along with actual sales orders and inventory levels to determine net production requirements.

Context

This process may be very effective for small to medium-sized companies with lower production levels and a limited number of SKUs. In fact, smaller companies might require a simpler architectural design in order to achieve this end-to-end process. But as previously mentioned, to optimize this process on a very large scale and seamlessly match unconstrained and constrained plans, incorporating SAP IBP would be optimal.

Components

  • SAP Analytics Cloud
  • SAP BTP Integration Suite
  • Planned Independent Requirement API
  • Planned Independent Requirement Help

entry.title: Don’t Miss Database Sessions at SAP TechEd 2024
entry.content:
SAP TechEd 2024 is right around the corner and includes a dynamic lineup of keynote presentations and interactive jumpstart sessions across SAP’s Data and Analytics portfolio, including SAP HANA Cloud. For database enthusiasts who enjoy solving business challenges and empowering users with top-tier tools, be sure to register for the sessions highlighted later in this blog.


A Short Note About the Next Generation of Applications

Chances are, anyone reading this article is likely familiar with using, maintaining, or dealing with legacy applications. As these systems age, their support costs grow while business needs evolve beyond the application’s original scope. These issues often leave IT decision-makers in a difficult position, constantly weighing the benefits of maintaining the status quo against the need for innovation. The unfortunate and simple truth regarding legacy applications is that they are no longer adequate for today’s business needs. To bridge this gap, IT teams must transition to intelligent data applications and utilize the full power of SAP's in-memory database. Features such as data-tiering, multi-model engines, and AI libraries enable today's custom solutions to handle a new generation of business challenges.

At SAP TechEd 2024, four feature presentations showcase intelligent data applications using SAP HANA Cloud as the database. Learn more about these virtual sessions below and make sure to register today!


DA101 | SAP HANA Cloud: Recent innovations, road map, and strategy 
Tuesday, Oct 8, 11:30 AM - 11:55 AM CET / 5:30 AM - 5:55 AM EST
Register  |  Watch Session Preview

As businesses innovate to meet evolving demands and drive efficiency, staying ahead of data infrastructure advancements is crucial. Join Thomas Hammer for an in-depth look at the latest innovations in SAP HANA Cloud, including new vector capabilities that integrate generative AI into enterprise solutions. This session also covers the latest road map and strategy, offering insights into creating a scalable, future-proof database platform with SAP HANA Cloud and SAP Business Technology Platform.


DA102 | AI-enabled clean core with SAP HANA Cloud 
Tuesday, Oct 8, 3:30 PM - 3:55 PM CET / 9:30 AM - 9:55 AM EST
Register  |  Watch Session Preview

Maintaining stable and consistent core ERP applications while driving innovation is a key challenge for many businesses today. In this session, Volker Saggau explains SAP’s clean core strategy and demonstrates how it helps global companies keep their ERP systems up-to-date and unmodified without comprising system integrity. With the support of SAP HANA Cloud’s multi-model and multi-tenancy capabilities, SAP customer Henkel shares how they balance system hygiene with innovation by leveraging SAP Business Technology Platform to power their $12 billion revenue environment.


DA103 | Generative AI-driven business transformation enabled by SAP HANA Cloud
Wednesday, Oct 9, 6:30 PM - 6:55 PM CET / 12:30 PM - 12:55 PM EST
Register  |  Watch Session Preview

As AI evolves, organizations need robust solutions for business transformation. Join Christoph Morgen and Markus Fath for a session that explores how SAP HANA Cloud supports the development of generative AI applications on the SAP Business Technology Platform. Key topics include multi-model capabilities like the vector engine, insights into SAP HANA’s AI function libraries, and a practical use case involving SAP Joule.


DA181 | Build AI-enabled intelligent data applications to help keep the core clean 
Wednesday, Oct 9, 7:30 PM - 7:55 PM CET / 1:30 PM - 1:55 PM EST
Register  |  Watch Session Preview

In the pursuit of innovation, organizations should ensure their core ERP systems remain unmodified and consistent. This session discusses the concept of clean core and its role in supporting custom applications alongside their ERP systems. Key topics include how SAP HANA Cloud powers the development of AI-enabled intelligent data applications using its vector engine as well as the upcoming knowledge graph engine. The session also highlights how code generation within SAP Build speeds up the development process.


Build and Deploy Intelligent Data Applications Today

Learn more about SAP HANA Cloud. Supplement these SAP TechEd sessions with first-hand experience using the SAP HANA Cloud free basic trial. Click here to get started.

entry.title: What’s new with Joule in the upcoming SAP SuccessFactors 2H 2024 release
entry.content:
Joule recently celebrated its first birthday and it’s amazing how much it has grown since being introduced just one year ago. Over the last 12 months, SAP has continuously enhanced Joule’s copilot capabilities with innovations that improve the conversational experience and increase the value the solution can bring to our customers. Many of the enhancements delivered so far and many more on the solution roadmap are the result of some specific end-user expectations of what a copilot should deliver:

  1. People expect Joule to deliver a more “ChatGPT-like” experience
  2. People expect a smoother conversational experience from a copilot
  3. People are eager to talk to Joule in additional languages beyond English

Let’s review some of the ways Joule is growing to meet these expectations from our SAP SuccessFactors community in our upcoming release.

Enhanced conversational experience and new Joule use cases

In this release, we are introducing Joule Functions, a new conversational experience that enables smarter, more adaptive conversations by managing dialog flows, improving language understanding, and keeping track of chat history and user context for seamless interactions. What can users expect from this new conversational experience?

  • Joule users will enjoy more natural, human-like conversations with more accurate intent recognition.
  • Users can now more easily switch between topics while keeping chat history and context intact.
  • Joule will provide more precise answers tailored to your previous interactions and context.

For example, as shown in the images below, if a user asks to generate feedback to send to an employee, Joule will retain the context of the conversation, allowing the user to request additional updates to the generated content before it is sent. Rather than following predefined paths, Joule responds even more intelligently to the user’s needs, offering a fluid and natural conversational experience.

 

 Figure 1 – An example of Joule’s new enhanced conversational experience

The following new transactional use cases are included in this release and take advantage of the new conversational experience:

  • AI-assisted onboarding task completion - Helps improve the efficiency of HR Partners and Managers by completing onboarding tasks via Joule.
  • View performance and development goals - Employees can now efficiently access a list of performance and development goals, as well as review goal details, by using conversational chat in Joule.

 Figure 2 – An example of AI-assisted onboarding task completion via Joule

Additional new navigational use cases that take advantage of the new conversational experience are included in this release, including the ability to assist new hires during onboarding as well as the ability to view information from Opportunity Marketplace, Growth Portfolio, and job requisitions.

All of the aforementioned new Joule use cases will be available with the production release on November 15, 2024. After that date, we will resume delivering new Joule features and use cases on a monthly basis and we will also continue to issue the associated What’s New Viewer (WNV) documents each month. It’s important to note that the WNV will not reflect these new items until November 15.

A total of 70 existing Joule use cases are being upgraded to take advantage of the new conversational experience. It's important to note that the HR Policy FAQ use case has already been upgraded for the new conversational experience and is available for use today. Please see the Appendix in this blog for a complete list of the Joule use cases that are being upgraded in conjunction with the 2H 2024 release. All use cases (whether transactional or navigational) not upgraded in this release will be upgraded in the monthly Joule releases beginning in December 2024.

New Joule data center strategy

To make it easier for customers to adopt Joule, we have enhanced the Joule data center consumption strategy. This change introduces flexibility by allowing customers to determine which Joule data center they wish to use. In addition, this change supports global customers or those with complex landscapes who choose to integrate Joule with multiple SAP business applications on different data centers, even across different regions. SAP will continue to introduce new Joule data centers in other regions and will always consider unique customer migration needs.

Please refer to the Joule product document Data Centers Supported by Joule to understand the Joule data center details as well as the instruction on how to determine a Joule data center.

An update on Joule language availability

Four critical languages – German, Spanish, French, and Portuguese – will be supported in Joule beginning in early 2025. Several other additional languages like Japanese, Korean, Dutch, and Chinese are planned to follow in the Joule roadmap (delivery timelines not available as of this writing).

Summary

As mentioned above, it is important to remember that all new Joule features and use cases will be available to customers beginning with the 2H production release on November 15, 2024. This release brings an innovative new conversational experience to Joule, as well as several new Joule use cases, a new Joule data center strategy, and updates to the ongoing language rollout for Joule.

Finally, some SAP SuccessFactors Joule content such as access to profile, feedback, approvals, and HR policy questions will also be accessible via the SAP Mobile Start app beginning in late November, 2024. More details on that to come!

Appendix

List of 70 Joule use cases upgraded to the new conversational experience in 2H 2024 release:

  • Compensation  |  View award balance
  • Compensation  |  View compensation forms
  • Employee Central  |  View addresses
  • Employee Central  |  View email addresses

entry.title: Breaking Out of Comfort Zones: The Learning Journey of Danielle Larocca
entry.content:
There is no doubt that the way we learn today has changed – dramatically. “I remember what it was like to attend SAP training classes back in the late 90’s”, Danielle Larocca recounts, “back then they were hosted onsite only and you would get paper-based certificates via a small ceremony each time.”  

It was 1995, and for Danielle, her SAP HCM and Payroll reporting experience was just about to start. She was working for Doubleday Direct, which later became a Bertelsmann AG and AOL Time Warner Partnership in Garden City, New York. The company had decided to implement SAP amidst fears that their legacy payroll system would cease functioning in the Y2K era. And as part of the implementation project, she was sent off for ABAP/4 training.  

Learning all about AI

Today, the learning experience looks very different for the global VP for Strategic Alliances. “Over the past several months, I have been dedicating my educational hours to all things AI.” What else would it be in the year 2024? The SAP Mentor started writing a series of blogs, ranging from AI-generated headshots, to functional video avatars and video language conversions. Her work – and learning experience – has become way more digital, fast-paced, and versatile compared to previous decades. “I have even let AI write a blog post for me and make a ppt for me", she highlights. “I also make an effort to share a weekly #AI Byte on LinkedIn that shares with others some key topics on AI that should be on their radar.” No doubt that the way she learns and the topics she learns about have changed.  

But there is more to is, says Danielle: “My life has changed in the sense that I have gone outside my comfort zone.” Moving away from HCM, Payroll, and reporting – her familiar turf – Danielle started to learn about technologies outside of her world, “to expand my horizon on the art of the possible with what technology can do.” Her latest achievement: The “Introducing SAP Business AI Commercial Model” learning journey on SAP Learning site, which rewarded her with an official record of achievement to demonstrate her expertise. 

Adapting to changing technologies is important

However, Danielle admits that learning a new skill sometimes comes with a small damper: “It is hard to A) carve out the time and B) learn something new but it is rewarding and exciting to be in the loop on the latest technological advancements that will transform our everyday lives.” As we get older, she states, “it's easy to settle into our homeostasis of what we know and what we are experts on and it's easy to stay in that comfort zone, but it does not allow for personal growth.” According to Danielle, we all must adapt and learn new things to survive and thrive, especially in an ever-changing technological evolution climate.   

“With all that said I have carved out time from my very busy schedule to learn something new each week.  I call it my education hours and they usually fall on a Sunday night.  I am committed to learning something new for 2 hours every week that is outside the scope of my day-to-day life and it involves some form of cutting-edge tech, for me its AI.” 

How do you learn? Share!

How do you tackle learning a new skill? Let us know about your strategy in the comments and start your next chapter of boosting your career with future-focused learning on the SAP Learning site today.

entry.title: 2H 2024 Release Highlights : Unlocking the Power of AI in SAP SuccessFactors
entry.content:
AI in Action

Artificial Intelligence is no longer just a buzzword—it’s reshaping how we work, especially in HR. SAP SuccessFactors continues to revolutionize HR technology with its AI-powered features in the 2H 2024 release. Artificial intelligence is seamlessly integrated to enhance productivity, personalization, and decision-making. Here’s a comprehensive look at the AI-driven functionalities that are making waves across multiple modules.

1. AI-Assisted Microsoft Teams Chat Integration

  • Module: Platform

SAP SuccessFactors introduces an AI-driven chat integration with Microsoft Teams, designed to streamline communication. The AI assists by providing contextually relevant responses and suggestions, making collaboration more efficient and user-friendly. With this feature, users can initiate quick actions and complete tasks simply by typing natural-language requests. The feature supports multiple locales and recognizes different phrases with the same intention. For example, whether users type "I want to request a day off" or "request OOO" or something else with the same meaning, the app will return the Request Time off quick action to help them proceed.

AI-Assisted Microsoft Teams App Chat, is now available in Work Tech

 

2. AI-Assisted Skill Recommendations from Continuous Performance Management

  • Module: Performance & Goals / Platform

Using data from Continuous Performance Management (CPM), Talent Intelligence Hub can now use AI-assisted capabilities to infer and recommend skills to employees in their Growth Portfolio. This ensures employees are always aware of the skills they should focus on to enhance their growth and development.

Talent Intelligence Hub can now use AI-assisted capabilities

Talent Intelligence Hub can now use AI-assisted capabilities

 

3. AI-Assisted Skills Standardization

  • Module: Platform

We can now use AI-assisted capabilities to use the industry-standard skill names in the Attributes Library. Consistency is key, and SAP SuccessFactors addresses this by leveraging AI to standardize skill names across job profiles, resumes, and talent pools. This feature minimizes redundancy and ensures uniformity in skill tracking.

TIH Skills Standardization

 

4. AI-Assisted Career Insights

  • Module: Succession & Development

In Career Development, now leveraging generative AI capabilities, employees can gain insights into development actions they can take towards their target role. AI technology into the Career Worksheet feature, can unlock a wealth of knowledge and recommendations to accelerate their career development journey.

Boost your career with AI-powered insights

 

5. AI-Assisted Writing for Message to Recipient of a Spot Award

  • Module: Compensation

When sending messages for Spot Awards, AI assists managers by generating impactful message templates. This feature not only saves time but ensures that communications are professional and motivating. When you select the AI tool after selecting any text in the Message to Recipient text box, AI-assisted writing options other than the Generate Message are available to use.

AI-assisted writing features for reward recipient message

 

6. Enhanced AI-Assisted Writing in 360 Reviews

  • Module: Performance & Goals

The 360-degree feedback process benefits from AI-driven writing assistance, enabling reviewers to provide structured and constructive comments. This is especially useful for providing comprehensive feedback with minimal effort. Two action items are now accessible from the context menu of that tool: Improve My Feedback & Generate Comments.

AI-assisted writing tool to help generate comments and improve feedback

 

7. AI-Assisted Insights into Detailed 360 Report

  • Module: Performance & Goals

The AI in 360 reviews didn't stop at writing— it also offers detailed insights into 360 review reports. By analyzing patterns and data, AI helps managers make better-informed decisions about performance and talent development. The generative AI in the Detailed 360 Report can be used in two scenarios: Overall understanding of a Detailed 360 Report & Consolidated analysis of a specific skill or competency.

Overall Insights for 360 Report

 

8. Enhanced AI-Assisted Writing in Opportunity Marketplace Assignments

  • Module: Opportunity Marketplace

In Opportunity Marketplace, assignment owners and co-owners can now create and edit assignments using generative AI capabilities. This feature generates relevant assignments based on organizational needs, allowing for optimal talent allocation. When assignment owners or co-owners use the updated Enhance Writing action, it now considers the following context in addition to the existing description text:

  • Assignment type
  • Assignment title
  • Job role defined for the assignment when job role is enabled for assignments
  • Attributes added for the assignment

AI-Assisted Writing in OMP

 

9. Enhancements to AI-Assisted Goal Creation

  • Module: Performance & Goals / Succession & Development

As you know Goal Management module features an AI-assisted goal creation tool.SAP made several enhancements to AI-assisted goal creation in the latest Goal Management. Enhancements includes:

  • Updated AI tag : "AI-generated content", replacing the previous "AI-enhanced content"
  •  Streamlined content comparison: AI-assisted writing tool is now in place to enhance content directly at the field level. Compare Generated Content page has been removed.
  • Improved prompt template: prompt template is now optimized to automatically apply goal plan configurations, such as start, due, and maxlength, to AI-generated goals
  • Support for custom fields: Custom fields of text and textarea types are now supported for performance goals. By providing meaningful text for <field-description>, you specify the context, which in turn helps improve the quality of AI-generated content.

AI-Assisted Goal Creation

 

 

10. AI-Assisted Job Skills on Career Sites

  • Module: Recruiting

Recruiting has been enhanced with AI-driven skill matching on career sites. This feature helps highlight the most relevant skills for job postings, ensuring companies attract the right candidate. With this enhancement, you can now add a Skills Cloud component to the Job layout in Career Site Builder, so that job skills are displayed on your career site in the form of a word cloud.

AI-Assisted Skills Cloud

 

11. AI-Assisted Skills Matching for Candidate Resumes

  • Module: Recruiting

AI also plays a crucial role in resume screening by matching candidates' resumes with job requisitions. This speeds up the recruitment process and ensures that only the best-fit candidates move forward. When the AI-Assisted Skills Matching for Resumes feature is enabled, the search results page on career sites displays the option to upload a resume. You have the option to present the candidate with a data privacy consent statement (DPCS) before they upload it. After they accept the DPCS and upload their resume, we use AI to analyze the resume and identify their skills. Then, we recommend jobs that match their skills.

AI-Assisted Skills Matching for Resumes

 

12. AI-Assisted Skills Validation for Job Requisitions

  • Module: Recruiting

Before job postings go live, AI ensures that all required skills are validated. This prevents mismatches between job requirements and candidate qualifications, making the recruitment process more efficient. Recruiters can perform the following tasks to enhance the skills listed in the job requirements:

  • Add, remove, and save skills in the job requisition to control the visibility of the skills listed in the job posting on the career site.
  • Categorize primary and secondary skills to find candidates who best fit the job based on their skills matching the skills specified in the job requirements.

Skills Validation for Job Requisitions

 

13. Enhanced AI-Assisted Writing Across Modules

  • Module: Across Suite

The Text Analyzer, a new AI-enhanced feature, brings advanced capabilities such as translating text and analyzing for biases. It scans for potential age, gender, and cultural biases, ensuring inclusivity and fairness in communications .

The following modules are adopting AI-assisted writing for the first time in the 2H 2024 release:

  • Compensation
  • Rewards and Recognition
  • Variable Pay
  • Calibration
  • Employee Central
  • Platform
  • Learning
  • Onboarding
  • Dynamic Teams (including Objectives and Key Results)
  • Latest People Profile
  • Talent Intelligence Hub

Text Analyzer scans for the following safety attributes like age bias, gender bias, etc.

AI-Assisted Writing & Translations

 

14. AI in Learning: Use AI to Associate Skills With Learning Items

  • Module: Learning

The Learning module benefits from AI, which associates relevant skills with learning content automatically. This ensures employees are always learning the right skills for their roles. You can use the AI-assisted capabilities to associate skills with Open Content Network (OCN) items based on the predefined skills provided by the OCN providers and thru imports for all learning items.

Learning Items - Skills Inference

 

 

This comprehensive list of AI features captures the essence of the 2H 2024 release and its significant impact on HR management. These innovations making sure that employees and managers can focus on what truly matters: growth, development, and success. AI isn’t just changing how we work—it’s making HR more human.

Are you ready to embrace the future of work?

Driving HR Innovation with Artificial Intelligence

entry.title: Vector Indexing for Retrieval-Augmented Generation with Databricks, SAP HANA, and Generative AI Hub
entry.content:
Introduction

In the rapidly evolving landscape of artificial intelligence, the combination of vector indexing and retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) has emerged as a powerful approach for enhancing the accuracy and relevance of generated content. By leveraging the capabilities of Databricks, SAP HANA Database, and tools for Generative AI like the Generative AI Hub SDK and LangChain, we can create a robust framework for efficient and scalable vector indexing.
In my previous blog post, we saw how LangChain could be used with SAP HANA for vector indexing and RAG. In this blog post, we will explore the step-by-step process of setting up a vector indexing pipeline using Databricks for data processing, SAP HANA as the vector database, and the Generative AI Hub SDK alongside LangChain for seamless integration. At each step, code examples for a Databricks notebook will be provided. Whether you're a data scientist, AI researcher, or a developer looking to enhance your RAG workflows, this guide will provide you with the insights and practical knowledge needed to get started. Let's dive into the intricacies of this powerful combination and unlock new potentials for your AI-driven projects.

Setting Up Our Environment

Databricks clusters come with a good number of pre-installed packages for data science, data engineering and AI purposes, so tools like Spark and LangChain are already available by default. However, there are some packages that we will need for the example pipeline that we will be building:
  • html2text: For converting text from HTML to Markdown format
  • generative-ai-hub-sdk: For working with Generative AI models in the Generative AI Hub
  • hdbcli: For connection to a HANA Database

The following code snippet could be executed in Databricks to install these packages:

 

%pip install html2text "generative-ai-hub-sdk[all]" hdbcli

dbutils.library.restartPython()

 

 

Note: While generative-ai-hub-sdk and hdbcli are required for the vector indexing process, html2text is only required for this example. Your use cases might not necessarily require html2text, but might require other packages instead, so you should adjust the pip install command accordingly to fit your use cases.

Building Our Vector Indexing Pipeline

Building a vector indexing pipeline is essentially a data engineering task, and as such, we could split the pipeline processes into the Extract, Transform, and Load (ETL) steps.

Extract

The extract step involves ingesting raw data from one or more data sources. For our example, our raw data are HTML files exported from an Atlassian Confluence wiki space, where each HTML file contains the contents of a single wiki page. These files are stored in a container within an Azure Data Lake Service (ADLS) account, and can be read using PySpark as shown:

 

from pyspark.sql.functions import expr

HTML_FILEPATHS_GLOB_PATTERN = "abfss://aitm@coredatalaketestint.dfs.core.windows.net/sandbox/example_wikis/*"

html_binary_spark_df = (
    spark
    .read
    .format("binaryFile")
    .options(pathGlobFilter="*.html")
    .load(HTML_FILEPATHS_GLOB_PATTERN)
    .withColumn("content", expr("CAST(content AS STRING)"))
)

 

 

If the extract step goes well, we should get a PySpark DataFrame containing these columns:
  • path: the full paths to the HTML files, which look something like abfss://aitm@coredatalaketestint.dfs.core.windows.net/sandbox/example_wikis/GAI01.03.10-RAGe_4337208805.html
  • modificationTime: timestamp values indicating when the files were last modified
  • length: length of the file contents
  • content: decoded HTML contents of the files

 

Note:
  • The snippet above shows how we can ingest data that exists as raw binary files in ADLS, but your use cases might involve data in different formats (e.g. CSV, Parquet) or from different sources (e.g. database, API), so you will need to adjust your ingestion logic accordingly. The documentation for working with inputs and outputs using PySpark is a good place to figure out how you could use Spark for ingesting your data.
  • In this example, HTML_FILEPATHS_GLOB_PATTERN points to a container in ADLS where the raw data is stored, with aitm being the container name, coredatalaketestint.dfs.core.windows.net being the address of the ADLS instance, and sandbox/example_wikis/ being the folder within the aitm container where the data is stored. For different cloud platforms (GCP, AWS etc), the URI would be different, so it is advised to check the relevant documentations from Spark or the respective cloud platforms.

Transform

After we have ingested our data, the next step is to clean and preprocess our data into a suitable format for vector indexing. For this example, we will perform the following to preprocess our data:
  • Converting the contents from HTML to Markdown: Markdown is syntactically simpler compared to HTML, which makes it more understandable to Large Language Models (LLMs), thus we do this conversion
  • Removing unnecessary contents: We will remove headers and footers in page contents to reduce noise in the data, which could help improve the retrieval performance of a RAG application. We also remove authors from the contents for privacy reasons.
  • Getting the base file names: This is an optional step to get the base file names from the path values in the path column, for recording as metadata during the indexing step later.
These smaller steps can be done as shown:

 

import re
import html2text
from pyspark.sql.functions import udf, col
from pyspark.sql.types import StringType

AUTHORS_REGEX_PATTERN = re.compile(r"Created\sby.*last\smodified.*[A-Z][a-z]{2}\s\d{2}\,\s\d{4}")

convert_html_to_markdown = udf(html2text.html2text, StringType())

@udf(StringType())
def strip_header_and_footer(content: str) -> str:
    return content[53:-100]

@udf(StringType())
def remove_authors(content: str) -> str:
    return AUTHORS_REGEX_PATTERN.sub("", content)

@udf(StringType())
def get_filename(path: str) -> str:
    return path.split("/")[-1]

wiki_texts_spark_df = (
    html_binary_spark_df
    .withColumn("content", convert_html_to_markdown(col("content")))
    .withColumn("content", strip_header_and_footer(col("content")))
    .withColumn("content", remove_authors(col("content")))
    .withColumn("filename", get_filename(col("path")))
    .selectExpr("filename", "content")
)

 

 

If the cleaning and preprocessing steps go well, we should get a different PySpark DataFrame containing these columns:
  • filename: the base name of the HTML files, which look something like GAI01.03.10-RAGe_4337208805.html
  • content: the cleaned contents of the wiki pages in Markdown format

 

Note: Spark user-defined functions (UDFs) can be used to apply preprocessing steps to the columns of a PySpark DataFrame in a simple and clean fashion. More information about PySpark UDFs can be found in the PySpark documentation.
After cleaning our data, we will still need to split our wiki contents into chunks. This is especially important if we are working with LLMs that have lower token limits. We also typically want to have smaller chunks of text to reduce the risk of encountering the lost-in-the-middle problem during the RAG process.
For our example, we use LangChain to load the PySpark DataFrame and perform the chunking of wiki contents:

 

from langchain_community.document_loaders import PySparkDataFrameLoader
from langchain_text_splitters import RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter

full_contents_loader = PySparkDataFrameLoader(spark_session=spark, df=wiki_texts_spark_df, page_content_column="content")
documents_to_split = full_contents_loader.load()

document_splitter = RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter.from_tiktoken_encoder(
    model_name="gpt-4",
    chunk_size=200,
    chunk_overlap=20
)

document_chunks = document_splitter.split_documents(documents_to_split)

 

 

Note: While we use the RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter class from LangChain with chunk_size of 200 and chunk_overlap of 20 as an example, other chunking strategies might work better for your own use cases. Therefore, some exploration should be done to determine chunking strategies that work better for your use cases. For a starting place on chunking strategy, you can refer to this Medium post.

Load

Now that we have cleaned and preprocessed our data, we are ready for the indexing step itself.
As we are using an embedding model from the Generative AI Hub as well as the HANA Database for the vector indexing step, we need to load the secrets for these services. For this example, these secrets have been stored as Databricks secrets which can be loaded as shown:

 

import json

SECRET_SCOPE = "TEST_AITM_SCOPE"
gen_ai_hub_service_key = json.loads(dbutils.secrets.get(scope=SECRET_SCOPE, key="EXAMPLE_GENAI_HUB_SERVICE_KEY"))
hana_secrets = json.loads(dbutils.secrets.get(scope=SECRET_SCOPE, key="EXAMPLE_HANA_VECTOR_SECRETS"))

 

After loading the secrets, we want to initialize the client objects for Generative AI Hub and HANA Database respectively as shown:

 

# HANA DB
from hdbcli import dbapi

hana_conn = dbapi.connect(
    address=hana_secrets["host"],
    port=hana_secrets["port"],
    user=hana_secrets["user"],
    password=hana_secrets["password"],
    autocommit=True,
    sslTrustStore=hana_secrets["certificate"],
)


# GenAI Hub
import os
from gen_ai_hub.proxy.core.proxy_clients import get_proxy_client

os.environ["AICORE_AUTH_URL"] = gen_ai_hub_service_key["url"]
os.environ["AICORE_CLIENT_ID"] = gen_ai_hub_service_key["clientid"]
os.environ["AICORE_CLIENT_SECRET"] = gen_ai_hub_service_key["clientsecret"]
os.environ["AICORE_RESOURCE_GROUP"] = gen_ai_hub_service_key["appname"].split("!")[0]
os.environ["AICORE_BASE_URL"] = f"{gen_ai_hub_service_key['serviceurls']['AI_API_URL']}/v2"

proxy_client = get_proxy_client("gen-ai-hub")

 

We then want to initialize the LangChain objects for working with our embedding model and HANA DB:

 

from gen_ai_hub.proxy.langchain.init_models import init_embedding_model
from langchain_community.vectorstores import HanaDB

embeddings = init_embedding_model("text-embedding-ada-002", proxy_client=proxy_client)
hana_vectordb = HanaDB(embedding=embeddings, connection=hana_conn, table_name="DATABRICKS_HANA_EXAMPLE_VECTORSTORE")

 

Note: My previous blog post gives more explanation of this code snippet, so you may want to check it out.

The last bit of the indexing process is a simple method call to write the chunks to HANA DB. LangChain will take care of the embedding of chunks and writing to HANA under the hood.

 

hana_vectordb.add_documents(document_chunks)

 

Optionally, we can run the following code snippet to test the retrieval of document chunks, as a sanity check:

 

hana_vector_retriever = hana_vectordb.as_retriever()

hana_vector_retriever.get_relevant_documents("What is RAG?")

 

 

What Next?

Now that we have our pipeline notebook, the typical next step would be to make our pipeline scalable by creating a Databricks job that runs that notebook. Configurations such as trigger schedules or notifications depend on the use case and can be set in Databricks accordingly. More information can be found in the Databricks documentation for jobs.
The vectors that are indexed can be consumed by a Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) application to enhance the relevance and accuracy of generated content. For an example of how to consume these vectors using LangChain, refer to my previous blog post where I demonstrate the integration and utilization of these vectors in a RAG workflow.

Conclusion

In this blog post, we've delved into the intricate process of setting up a vector indexing pipeline using Databricks, SAP HANA, and Generative AI tools like the Generative AI Hub SDK and LangChain. By carefully walking through each step—from data extraction and transformation to loading and indexing—we've demonstrated how to build a scalable and efficient pipeline that significantly enhances the accuracy and relevance of AI-generated content. This powerful combination of technologies not only streamlines your RAG workflows but also opens new avenues for innovation in AI-driven projects.  Feel free to leave your comments and share your thoughts or questions about this blog post.
Happy coding and may your AI projects reach new heights of excellence! 🥂

entry.title: Lessons from the SAP Advisory Council for Waste & Recycling annual meeting
entry.content:
On September 25th  and 26th took place in Walldorf the annual presential meeting  of the SAP Advisory Council for Waste and Recycling is a,  by invitation only, a select group of companies  willing to collaborate in an open environment with SAP. With more of 25 editions, the Advisory Council is a well established forum whose members drive their companies digital vision and stablish a community beyond the physical meetings with the following purposes.

​- Feedback and validation

​- Collaboration

​- Communication

​- Product adoption information exchange

 ​Image:  SAP Advisory Council for Waste & Recycling meeting atendees

​This year edition attended more than 25 delegates from 9 companies plus  Prologa, in the condition of developer of strategic industry cloud solutions for the Waste and Recycling industry and Gruler & Cleve Consulting  which accepted the invitation to share their experiences and help conducting a design thinking workshop.

Agenda for this year was divided in three parts:  product strategy and development updates, ideation workshop on Artificial Intelligence and conversion experiences into S/4HANA.

​SAP  product strategy and development updates were presented by Solution Management and Product Management teams (Image:  Uwe Zachmann and Somik Sarkar, Waste industry responsibles from Product Management team) which offered their respective perspectives, this is from extending the scope of current solution into the recycling part of the value chain to a detailed description and demonstration of the new developments released for the SAP for Waste & Recycling  part of S/4HANA plus insights of the ongoing and future developments.  All these updates provided were explained in the context of the overall SAP product portfolio strategy around RISE offering, where Artificial Intelligence capabilities will play a key role.  In this sense, and as an introduction for the later comprehensive ideation session on this subject, a PoC of Jule showing a use case for the waste collection process was shown.

​Prologa presented very interested and relevant updates as well.  Of special relevance has been the introduction of Mr. Christoph Hillmann as Prologa VP for Circular Economy & CoDevelopment who made a vibrant presentation of their product strategy and the ongoing development roadmap for their components, with special focus in the Order Planning and Dispatching solution plus a spark of their updated go – to – market strategy which will include an event next november   

 

​Image:  Ideation session

​Second part of the meeting has been an ideation workshop on AI can help to develop waste & recycling business. Session was brillianty conducted by Isabel Creuznacher from Gruler & Cleve Consulting jointly with Henán Sendra from the Center of Expertise - CoE of SAP BTP.

​ Part one

  • Short Intro of objective WS
  • Inspiration overview:  AI for SAP
  • Intro Design Thinking as Method for WS

​Part two

  • Teambuilding
  • Exercise Needfinding Journey (participants):  discuss the journey and define the problems
  • Group presentations main pain points (participants)

 

​Part three

  • Split teams according to problem area
  • Intro Ideation Session
  • Ideation Sessions for use cases

oBrainstorming / “crazy 8” (individual)

oSelect for the best 10 ideas (group)

  • Selection of idea (ideation matrix for feasibility)
  • Wrap-up

​Third part of the meeting was dedicated to the conversion experiences of SAP ECC systems into S4HANA.  A very big thank you to our co-chairs Roman Aiyer from WBD - Duisburg and Matthias Reich from Prezero for the great feedback on their S4HANA conversion projects with the great help of Mr. Jürgen Krist.

​.We are confident that more successful and interesting sessions will come in the future. Noticing that members play an active part in the orchestration and conduction of activities is a positive signal.  For all involved SAP departments, the value of the Advisory Council is undisputed.

 

​

entry.title: [sunset] Business Entity Recognition is getting deprecated 02/25
entry.content:
Revisiting Business Entity Recognition (BER):

As part of the SAP AI Services portfolio, Business Entity Recognition was introduced with a clear goal: to improve text document processing by detecting and highlighting any type of named entity in unstructured text and sorting into predefined categories. This was achieved using advanced artificial intelligence and natural language processing technologies.
Key features included:
  • Extract Text Entity - Pre-trained Model: Customers could use pre-trained machine learning models to detect and highlight, in unstructured text, business entities that belong to predefined categories.
  • Extract Text Entity - Custom Model: Customers could create their own custom machine learning models to detect and highlight any type of business entity in unstructured text.
